retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Evaluation -- Genomic (Somatic) Tumor Biomarkers (Post-Biopsy)
retrieved_docs.content: Decipher test: This test evaluates tissue for the expression of 22 RNA biomarkers to calculate the probability of clinical metastasis within 5 years of definitive therapy, the prostate cancer-specific mortality at 10 years, the 5-year risk of developing metastases, and the chances of finding high-grade disease after radical prostatectomy. The test aims to help avoid overtreatment by reclassifying men originally identified as high-risk who are unlikely to develop metastatic disease and might safely avoid salvage radiation therapy after radical prostatectomy surgery. This test is most useful for higher-risk patients with localized disease who have already undergone radical prostatectomy and are potential candidates for salvage radiation and androgen deprivation therapy. Studies have demonstrated that 60% of men considered high-risk after surgery were reclassified to a lower-risk category following a genomic classifier designed to predict the development of distant metastases after surgical treatment of their prostate cancer. Salvage radiation therapy was safely avoided in 50% of the high-risk patients tested, and 98.5% of patients identified as low-risk by genomic testing did not develop metastases within 5 years of their radical prostatectomy procedures. [140] [212] Although the Decipher test can be used for any patient with localized disease, low- to intermediate-risk cancers, and a 10-year life expectancy, it is most helpful in higher-risk individuals, those with positive surgical margins, and patients with extraprostatic extension (T3), where it can help decide on the use of adjuvant radiation and hormonal therapy. [212] Similar to the Prolaris test, it can also be used in high-risk diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Evidence demonstrates similar outcomes for patients treated with active surveillance, surgery, and radiotherapy, although patient wishes and side effect profiles should be considered. Patients choosing monitoring strategies should be aware of a small increase in the risk of progression and that subsequent radical treatment will be required in around 50% of cases, as often for patient anxiety as for biochemical, clinical, or imaging evidence of disease progression. [22] [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Deferred Curative Strategy
retrieved_docs.content: Recent evidence has demonstrated survival benefits from radiotherapy plus ADT in men who would have been traditionally assigned to watchful waiting when they progress to locally advanced disease. This calls into question the distinction between active surveillance and watchful waiting. For instance, although the risk-benefit analysis at initial presentation with the localized disease might be considered unfavorable in an asymptomatic man with co-morbidities, progression to a more advanced stage (eg, T3 disease from T2) could trigger a more radical approach. This is in line with deferred curative treatment. As such, the key to successful monitoring of localized prostate cancer rests on thorough clinical evaluation and an honest appraisal, with the patient, of goals and priorities. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Low risk: PSA less than 10 ng/ml and stage T1-T2a and grade group 1 High risk: PSA less than 20 or grade group 4-5 Intermediate risk: constitutes a variety of combinations falling between low and high risk

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Localized Disease
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of localized prostate cancer, treatment selection generally has minimal impact on OS for at least the next 10 years. Therefore, definitive therapy should only be offered to patients who are reasonably expected to live another 10 years or longer based on age and comorbidities. [123]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Deferred Curative Strategy
retrieved_docs.content: Repeat biopsy is utilized where there are discordant findings between biomarkers and imaging. Radical treatment is offered if evidence of disease progression is present or if the patient requests it. Large randomized controlled trials, such as the ProtecT trial, have demonstrated no detriment to 10-year survival from offering active surveillance over primary radical treatment. The trade-off for the patient is an increased risk of disease progression against the adverse effects of radical treatment. [6] [37] [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Emergent Focal Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: As yet, studies for these treatments are confined to short and intermediate-term follow-up. They appear to have a vastly lower rate of high-grade complications. However, the long-term oncological results are uncertain, particularly given a relatively high rate of residual or recurrent cancer on repeat biopsy. At present, as promising as the results appear from the perspective of preserving the quality of life, they should only be offered as part of a clinical trial. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Natural history studies suggest that the majority of men, particularly elderly men diagnosed with localized prostate cancer, die with their disease rather than of it, reflecting competing hazards associated with aging. Although many cases do not progress, there does appear to be a true progression rate over the years of follow-up. Both grade and volume of the disease are predictive of progression to locally advanced and metastatic disease. As such, monitoring strategies are most suited to elderly men, low-risk diseases, or selected intermediate-risk diseases with careful counseling and close follow-up. Two broad approaches to monitoring are generally described.

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Despite being common, prostate cancer has the highest 5-year survival of all cancers at 98%. As previously discussed, localized prostate cancer is frequently indolent and has an excellent prognosis even if left untreated. By definition, patients with high-grade, high-volume cancer are more likely to progress to locally advanced and metastatic disease, but risks can be mitigated by early detection and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Systemic Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Enzalutamide has also been recently improved in the biochemical-recurrent nonmetastatic setting. In patients with a biochemical recurrence post-definitive therapy for their prostate cancer (either surgery or radiation) with a PSA doubling time of 9 months, it was found that limited treatment with 9 months of enzalutamide improved metastasis-free survival [339] . Due to the heterogeneity in the clinical management of biochemical-recurrent prostate cancer, only time reveals the extent to which this approach is being adopted into standard practice.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Localized Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Therefore, for most patients with potentially curable, localized disease, good performance status, reasonably good quality of life, and greater than 10-year life expectancy, the choice of treatment should be an informed patient decision made after discussions including both urology (surgery) and radiation therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Surgical Oncology -- Salvage Radiation Therapy After Radical Prostatectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Alternatively, some patients with only limited positive margins or extracapsular extension may choose close monitoring and delay the salvage radiation therapy until there is a PSA spike, rise, or other evidence of disease progression. However, this risks developing distant metastases that could have been prevented with earlier radiation treatment. A genomic classifier designed to predict the development of distant metastases after surgical treatment of prostate cancer can help assess and reliably estimate an individual patient's relative risk in these situations. [273]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: With prompt and effective diagnosis and treatment, localized prostate cancer has a favorable prognosis for patients. Multidisciplinary team members must support patients and optimize oncological and nononcological outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The lack of good, minimally invasive curative therapies for a localized disease that are less expensive and better tolerated compared to definitive radiation therapy or radical surgery

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Evaluation -- Genomic (Somatic) Tumor Biomarkers (Post-Biopsy)
retrieved_docs.content: Oncotype Dx Prostate Score Test: This test measures 17 gene expressions and is an automated immunofluorescence-based assay. The Oncotype Dx Prostate Score Test calculates the patient's chances of having organ-confined disease after radical prostatectomy surgery using genomic information from the biopsy specimen. The test focuses on 4 specific areas of gene expression—the stromal response, androgen signaling, cellular proliferation, and organization. This test has been shown to change treatment recommendations by 18%, primarily through a reduction in radiation therapy of 33% and a 10% increase in active surveillance. [212] A similar increase in the selection of active surveillance using Oncotype Dx was also found in a complementary study in the Veterans Affairs healthcare system. [213] Oncotype Dx is best suited for patients with localized, low, or favorable intermediate-risk disease (Gleason 3+3=6 or Gleason 3+4=7), where either active surveillance or definitive primary therapy is a reasonable treatment option. [212] [214] [215] [216]

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: All patients with high-risk cancer should complete staging examinations looking for metastatic disease with a bone scan and cross-sectional abdominopelvic imaging. [6] [33] [34] The routine use of such imaging in intermediate-risk is controversial.

In addition, پروستاتا is noted as the location of اضطراب بروستاتي, reflecting that various prostatic disorders occur in this organ. Recognizing the prostate as the common site for both malignant and non‑malignant conditions highlights its clinical significance for diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

Each of these metastatic entities is linked to a distinct morphological description: “Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck” is associated with metastatic glandular carcinoma (سرطانة غدية نقيلية); “Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck” is associated with metastatic cancer (السرطان النقيلي); and “Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck” is both a subtype of squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق) and is associated with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية). This integrated framework supports accurate diagnosis, prognostication, and site‑specific therapeutic planning for patients with diverse malignant neoplasms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: illustrates that the thymus, an endocrine organ, can serve as a metastatic destination. Renal oncocytoma (الورم الأورمي الكلوي) and renal cell carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكلوية) are specific entities within cancer of the kidney and renal pelvis; partial or complete nephrectomy (استئصال الكلية) is the surgical intervention commonly associated with these renal tumors. Collectively, these interrelated concepts demonstrate that malignant cellular infiltrates and malignant or uncertain tumor cells define primary and secondary (metastatic) disease across multiple organ systems—lymph nodes, bone, skin, respiratory and intrathoracic organs, endocrine glands, thymus, breast, ovary, kidney, and pancreas—providing clinicians with a structured framework for cancer origin identification, staging, and targeted therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2262
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions frequently co‑occur with حصاة حالبية (ureteral stone), سرطـان البروستاتا النَّقيلي (metastatic prostate cancer), and فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد (benign prostatic hyperplasia). This pattern of co‑occurrence indicates that patients who present with nonspecific genitourinary complaints may also have underlying stone disease, advanced prostate malignancy, or benign prostatic enlargement, underscoring the need for thorough diagnostic assessment to identify and manage these potentially related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: The non‑hematologic malignant disease category (الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) encompasses a broad spectrum of primary and secondary cancers, including metastatic cancer (السرطان النقيلي), glandular prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا الغدي), squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق), lung carcinoma of non‑smoking cells (سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة الرائحة), metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية), and metastatic glandular carcinoma (سرطانة غدية نقيلية). Metastatic malignant neoplasms that involve the lymph nodes of the neck are classified as “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck” and are considered a specific form of metastasis to the neck (نقائل إلى الرقبة). This entity, together with “Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck,” “Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck,” and “Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck,” are all mapped to the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1913
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE GENITOURINARY SYSTEM provides a hierarchical framework in which cancer of prostate is positioned as a subcategory. Within the cancer of prostate category, the specific malignant prostate tumor grouping identified as أورام البروستاتا الخبيثة (SMQ) is included as a member. This structured classification helps organize prostate neoplasms for accurate diagnosis, research, and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions arise within the urinary system and frequently co‑occur with prostatic disorders, seminal calculi, and both localized and metastatic prostate cancer; they are also clinically associated with prostate cancer. The prostate is an organ that belongs to the urinary system and serves as the anatomic site for prostate‑related diseases, including metastatic prostate cancer. Prostatic disorders are situated in the prostate, and other male genital disorders are likewise located in the prostate, representing subcategories of prostatic disorders. These other male genital disorders are linked to screening activities. Together, these relationships emphasize that disturbances of the prostate and associated genitourinary symptoms are central to the presentation, co‑occurrence, and clinical management of prostate disease, particularly cancer, and underscore the importance of appropriate diagnostic screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2502
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck is classified as a secondary malignancy, reflecting the spread of a primary cancer to regional lymphatic tissue. Recognizing this metastatic involvement as a secondary malignancy is important for accurate staging, prognosis, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2639
retrieved_docs.content: Transrectal biopsy of prostate is classified within the broader category of prostate procedures (عمليات البروستاتا), representing a specific type of operation performed on the prostate gland.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate (prostata) is a key anatomical site within the male genital system and is the focus of specific diagnostic procedures that are applied when the prostate tissue has been partially excised. This organ serves as the anatomic location for metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي), linking the disease directly to the prostate tissue. In addition, the prostate is identified as the location of a broader category of prostatic disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي), which functions as a parent classification for other male genital disorders. These other male genital disorders are themselves situated in the prostate and are closely associated with screening activities (التحصّي). Together, these relationships highlight the central role of the prostate in both disease manifestation and diagnostic evaluation, emphasizing the importance of targeted procedures and screening in the management of prostate‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1106
retrieved_docs.content: The prostate (prostata) serves as the anatomic site for metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). A prostatic disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) is located within the prostate, and other male genital disorders are likewise situated in the prostate. These other male genital disorders are classified as a subcategory of the prostatic disorder (they “have parent” اضطراب بروستاتي) and are associated with screening or preventive measures (التحصّي). Recognizing that the prostate is the primary location for both metastatic prostate cancer and various male genital disorders, and that these disorders are linked to screening activities, underscores the importance of targeted prostate evaluation and appropriate preventive strategies in patient care.
